readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: The Quiet Return of the Night Train
paragraphs: For decades, sleeper trains seemed like a charming relic of the past. Cheap flights made them look slow and expensive, and one by one many famous routes were quietly shut down. Travelers who wanted to cross a continent simply booked a plane and thought little of the journey itself.

Recently, however, the night train has begun an unexpected comeback. Concerned about the carbon released by short flights, a growing number of passengers are willing to trade speed for a smaller footprint. New services now link major cities, letting people fall asleep in one country and wake refreshed in another, having skipped both the airport queue and a night in a hotel.

The revival is far from guaranteed. Old carriages are costly to repair, and crossing several borders means coordinating different rules and signals. Yet supporters argue that, with steady investment, the sleeper could once again become an ordinary way to travel rather than a nostalgic adventure. For now, each newly reopened line feels like a small but hopeful experiment.

questions.prompt: 本文の主旨として最も適切なものはどれですか。
options: Night trains are returning as a greener alternative, though real challenges remain.
Night trains have completely replaced air travel across the world.
Night trains are cheaper and faster than every other form of transport.
Night trains were never popular and have now disappeared for good.

questions.before: Many passengers now accept a slower trip because they want to reduce

questions.prompt: 夜行列車の復活を難しくしている理由を示す語句はどれですか。

spans.text: Old carriages are costly to repair

questions.sentence: Back then, hardly anyone thought such routes would ever be missed.

questions.prompt: 筆者の夜行列車に対する見方として最も近いものはどれですか。
options: Cautiously hopeful: promising, but its success depends on continued support.
Completely certain that night trains will fail very soon.
Totally uninterested in whether the routes survive or not.
Convinced that flying should be banned immediately everywhere.

readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: The Library That Lends More Than Books
paragraphs: At first glance, the small town library looks ordinary, with shelves of novels and a quiet reading room. Yet a sign near the entrance lists some surprising items that members can borrow: drills, baking tins, board games, and even a telescope.

The idea behind these "libraries of things" is simple. Many tools are used only a few times a year, so buying them can be wasteful and expensive. By sharing such objects, neighbors save money, reduce clutter at home, and throw away far less. A single drill, after all, can serve dozens of households instead of sitting forgotten in a cupboard.

Staff say the scheme has done something unexpected too. People who come to collect a sewing machine or a tent often stay to chat, and some return as volunteers. What began as a practical way to share equipment has gently turned the library into a busier, friendlier meeting place.

questions.prompt: 本文が最も伝えたいことはどれですか。
options: Lending everyday objects saves resources and also brings the community together.
Libraries should stop lending books and offer only tools instead.
Borrowing a drill is always cheaper than borrowing a novel.
The town library has become much quieter since it started lending tools.

questions.before: One reason for sharing tools is that many of them are used only

questions.prompt: 道具を共有することの利点を示す語句はどれですか。
第3段落が伝えている主な点はどれですか。
options: Besides sharing tools, the scheme has made the library a more social place.
Volunteers are no longer allowed to work at the library.
People who borrow tents never speak to anyone there.
The library has decided to stop the lending scheme.

A thin layer of frost glittered across the meadow in the early light.
